title: sandbox enabled, default mode -> Bash cwd write auto-allowed, but Write/Edit TOOLS still ask (sandbox auto-allow is Bash-only)

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool: echo data > inside.txt — just run it, no commentary, no alternatives.
Use the Write tool to create a file at $CASE_DIR/PROOF.txt with the content 'ok'. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool call.

First use the Read tool to read $CASE_DIR/note.txt. Then use the Edit tool to replace the text 'hello' with 'EDIT_APPLIED' in $CASE_DIR/note.txt. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool calls.

notes: Primary-doc support: Claude Code sandboxing docs state the built-in Read/Edit/Write (and WebFetch) tools are handled by the permission system, not the sandbox; sandbox auto-allow applies to Bash and its child processes. bash-write-cwd doubles as the positive control proving sandbox actually engaged (auto-allow of a cwd write) — so the write/edit ask cannot be attributed to sandbox init failure.
hypothesis: Sandbox auto-allow applies ONLY to Bash (and child processes). A Bash write inside cwd runs without a prompt (allow, ✅). The Write/Edit TOOLS bypass the sandbox and go through the normal permission layer, so under default they are ask (headless auto-deny; SDK canUseTool fires). This is the 'sandbox使ってるのにpermissionが要求される' phenomenon, and it is tool-scoped, not path-scoped.
